coredata.dc:title: Rapid adsorption and detection of copper ions in water by dual-functional ion-imprinted polymers doping with carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Qingyan
Huang, Chao
Ma, Shujuan
Gong, Bolin
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: The pollution of heavy metals such as copper ions to water body is still serious, and the discharge of sewage containing heavy metals into the environment will cause damage to soil and even human body. Herein, poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate) was applied as matrix, methacrylic acid (MAA) as monomer, diphenylsemicarbazide (DPC) as ligand. The spherical copper ion imprinted polymer (Cu-IIP-A) was synthesized by atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP). Yellowish fluorescent carbon dot (CD) containing amino groups was prepared from tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine and added to during the preparation of Cu-IIP. A new copper ion imprinted polymer was acquired and assigned as CD@Cu-IIP-A. For comparison, amorphous Cu-IIP particles were prepared with the same raw materials by bulk polymerization, which were named as Cu-IIP-B and CD@Cu-IIP-B, respectively. Under the same conditions, the dispersion of CD@Cu-IIP-A in water was much better than that of CD@Cu-IIP-B, and the maximum adsorption capacity reached 85.6 mg g−1, which was larger than that of CD@Cu-IIP-B (66.05 mg g−1), and only slightly lower than that of Cu-IIP-A (91.9 mg g−1). CD@Cu-IIP-A only takes 30 min to reach adsorption equilibrium and has a good fluorescence quenching effect on copper ion at 80–780 μmol L-1. These indicated that CD@Cu-IIP-A exhibited both fluorescence detection of CD and selective adsorption functions of IIP, which can satisfy both detection and efficient enrichment of copper ion in water.

head.ce:title: Rapid adsorption and detection of copper ions in water by dual-functional ion-imprinted polymers doping with carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pollution of heavy metals such as copper ions to water body is still serious, and the discharge of sewage containing heavy metals into the environment will cause damage to soil and even human body. Herein, poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate) was applied as matrix, methacrylic acid (MAA) as monomer, diphenylsemicarbazide (DPC) as ligand. The spherical copper ion imprinted polymer (Cu-IIP-A) was synthesized by atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP). Yellowish fluorescent carbon dot (CD) containing amino groups was prepared from tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine and added to during the preparation of Cu-IIP. A new copper ion imprinted polymer was acquired and assigned as CD@Cu-IIP-A. For comparison, amorphous Cu-IIP particles were prepared with the same raw materials by bulk polymerization, which were named as Cu-IIP-B and CD@Cu-IIP-B, respectively. Under the same conditions, the dispersion of CD@Cu-IIP-A in water was much better than that of CD@Cu-IIP-B, and the maximum adsorption capacity reached 85.6 mg g−1, which was larger than that of CD@Cu-IIP-B (66.05 mg g−1), and only slightly lower than that of Cu-IIP-A (91.9 mg g−1). CD@Cu-IIP-A only takes 30 min to reach adsorption equilibrium and has a good fluorescence quenching effect on copper ion at 80–780 μmol L-1. These indicated that CD@Cu-IIP-A exhibited both fluorescence detection of CD and selective adsorption functions of IIP, which can satisfy both detection and efficient enrichment of copper ion in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of global industrialization, an increasing water pollution has brought serious problems. Heavy metal is one of the most poisonous pollutants which may result in severe medical problems [1,2]. Consequently, finding effective and feasible ways to address water pollution is critical [3]. As a typical heavy metal, copper is widely utilized in battery, manufacturing, metal coating and finishing, metallurgical and chemical industries [4,5]. The wastewater by these industry containing a large number of heavy metal ions especially copper ions will not only cause serious pollution to the environment [6,7], but also lead to great harmfulness to the human body and even the nervous system [8–11]. At present, in industrial wastewater, copper ion can be treated by membrane filtration, ion exchange, adsorption, chemical precipitation, nanotechnology treatment, electrochemistry and advanced oxidation process. Among the commonly used methods, electrochemical treatment methods such as electrocoagulation, elector-floatation, and electrodeposition are efficient, but tend to receive less attention because of their high energy consumption [12–14]. Biological treatment techniques, despite their rapid development in recent years due to cheaper treatment of pollutants on a large scale, generally require a certain degree of biodegradability of the treated objects and are not fully suitable for treatment of heavy metals [15]. Thus, physical methods such as membrane filtration, ion exchange, and adsorption are more preferred. Adsorption method is an eye-catching and promising technique to remove metal ion because of its high efficiency, high selectivity and low cost [16–19]. As a new type of nanomaterials, carbon dot (CD) has attracted great attention since they were discovered because of their excellent properties [20–22], such as easy functionalization, green and easy synthesis route, excellent water solubility, good photostability, good biocompatibility, bright fluorescence and adjustable surface function [23–27]. Its excellent fluorescence detection ability has been widely utilized in the fields of optoelectronics, biological imaging, catalysis, sensing, drug delivery and so on [28,29]. The combination of CD detection and adsorption materials to prepare a new material which can not only detect but also remove heavy metal ion possess broad application prospect.

Based on molecular imprinting technique, great progress has been made on the development of ion imprinting polymer (IIP) [30–33]. IIP has specific recognition ability to target ions and resistance to physical factors (temperature, pH, organic solvents, etc.) [34,35]. In recent years, the composite materials prepared by combining imprinted polymer and CD exhibit several advantages of them and possess broad application prospects [36–38]. Our research group has fabricated a few imprinting materials and CD-based dual-functional materials [39–42]. Combining the advantages of imprinted materials and CD to prepare bifocal composites with specific adsorption and fluorescence detection is still the focus of our research. The preparation of IIP was developed by bulk polymerization using diphenylsemicarbazide (DPC) as ligand, possessing excellent performance [43,44]. However, the IIP prepared by them was still unable to avoid a few shortcomings of traditional bulk polymerization method such as poor specificity, grinding leading to a destruction of imprinting cavity, uncontrollable polymerization, low yield, uneven particle size, poor dispersion in water and so on [45], and lack efficient detection capabilities, which limit its application in water.

Herein, monodisperse poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate) (named poly(GMA-co-EDMA)) resin was selected as matrix, and spherical copper ion imprinted polymer (Cu-IIP-A) was synthesized via atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) by complexation of DPC with copper ion and hydrogen bond interaction between methacrylic acid (MAA) as monomer and DPC. For comparison, amorphous copper ion (Cu2+) imprinted polymer (noted as Cu-IIP-B) was prepared by conventional bulk polymerization. In order to prepare materials with both specific adsorption and fluorescence detection properties, yellowish CD containing amino groups were prepared with tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine as precursors and introduced in the preparation process of both Cu-IIP-A and Cu-IIP-B, resulting in two kinds of new dual-functional copper ion imprinted CD@Cu-IIP-A and CD@Cu-IIP-B, respectively. And their ability to adsorb and detect copper ion in aqueous solutions was tested.

Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), o-phenylenediamine, tryptophan, DPC, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EDMA), azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), methacrylic acid (MAA), 2-2′-bipyridine (Bpy) and CuBr were provided by Aladdin Industries Co. (Shanghai). Disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na), polyvinyl alcohol and sodium dodecyl sulfate were purchased from Ratu Technology Co. (Beijing), Ltd. Other chemical reagents were of analytical grade. Dialysis bags were purchased from Biovake (USA). Deionized water consumed in whole experiments was gotten from a Milli-Q system (Millipore Inc., Milford, MA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of yellow fluorescent CD containing amino groups
ce:section.ce:para: According to previous report [46], yellowish CD was prepared by one-step hydrothermal polymerization. Typically, 100 mg of tryptophan and 75 mg of o-phenylenediamine were placed in water. The mixture was kept at 160 °C for 7 h. After taking out the reactor and cooling it to room temperature naturally, the obtained CD was dialyzed by a dialysis bag (1000 Da) and concentrated by rotary evaporation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and modification of monodisperse poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microsphere
ce:section.ce:para: According to our previous report [47], monodisperse poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres (noted MAR) were prepared by one-step seeded swelling polymerization. Then 3 g of MAR were placed in 0.1 mol L-1 sulfuric acid solution and reacted at 60 °C for 6 h. The product was washed to neutral with distilled water. After vacuum drying, hydrolyzed poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres (named MAR-OH) were obtained. The dried MAR-OH microspheres (3 g) were dispersed in dichloromethane solvent, 2.4 mL of triethylamine and 2.4 mL of 2-bromoisobutyryl bromide were added successively under ice bath conditions, and then reacted at room temperature for 24 h. The products were washed with water and acetone. After vacuum drying, brominated poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres (named MAR-Br) were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of spherical Cu-IIP by ATRP
ce:section.ce:para: First, 42.5 mg of CuCl2·2H2O were placed in a solution of chloroform/methanol, 60.5 mg of DPC and 50 μL of concentrated CD were added into the copper ion solution with continuous stirring 30 min to generate Cu2+/DPC complex. Then 132 μL of MAA were added. After self-assemble at room temperature for 3 h, 0.75 g of brominated poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres, 0.058 g of Bpy and 0.9 mL of EDMA were added in a double-mouth flask. The system was sealed and continuously injected with nitrogen to remove oxygen under the condition of liquid nitrogen freezing. The 0.027 g of CuBr were quickly added under the condition of nitrogen, sealed, and refrigerated with liquid nitrogen for continuous deoxygenation. Then the mixture reacted at 60 °C for 10 h to finish polymerization. At the end of reaction, the product was washed with ethanol, EDTA-2Na, deionized water and 1 mol L-1 of hydrochloric acid to remove copper ions thoroughly, then washed to neutral with distilled water, and the imprinted polymer was obtained and named CD@Cu-IIP-A after vacuum drying for 10 h.
Except that the CD was not added and the usage of DPC was increased to 121 mg, the rest of experimental steps remain unchanged, and the product was named Cu-IIP-A.
Except that the template copper ion was not added, the other experimental steps were the same as those for preparing Cu-IIP-A, and the obtained product was named NIP-A.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amorphous Cu-IIP by bulk polymerization
ce:section.ce:para: In order to compare the adsorption properties in water, amorphous IIPs were prepared by conventional bulk polymerization. First, 85.2 mg of CuCl2 2H2O were placed in a solution of chloroform/methanol, 121 mg of DPC and 100 μL concentrated CD were added into the copper ion solution with continuous stirring 30 min to produce Cu2+/DPC complex. Then 264 μL of MAA were added. After self-assembling at room temperature for 3 h, 2.16 mL of EDMA as cross-linking agent and 100 mg of AIBN as free-radical initiator were added with stirring. After transferring to an ampoule, the solution was purged thoroughly with N2 for 15 min. The ampoule was sealed with butane flame gun after vacuum and kept at 60 °C in water bath for 24 h to carry out polymerization. After that, the obtained polymers were ground in a bowl and sieved after drying, the synthesized polymers were washed with methanol to remove unreacted materials and 1 mol L-1 of HCl solution to leaching the imprinted metal ions until no copper ion could be detected in the eluent, and finally washed to pH 7 with distilled water and dried for 12 h to get the final CD@Cu-IIP-B.
Except that the CD was not added and the usage of DPC was increased to 242 mg, the rest of experimental steps remain unchanged, and the product was named Cu-IIP-B.
Except that the template copper ion was not added, the other experimental steps were the same as those of preparing Cu-IIP-B, and the obtained product was named NIP-B.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Carl Zeiss Jena, Sigma 500, GER) was observed to record the morphology of material. The surface functional groups of the adsorbent were determined by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Nicolet iS50 spectrometer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250XI XPS spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The specific surface area was calculated via the Brunner-Emmet-Teller model (BET, Quantachrome Nova 2000e, USA). The steady state and transient fluorescent spectrometer (RF, Fluoro Max-4, USA) measured its fluorescence emission spectrum. The residual concentration was analyzed with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV, TU-1950, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD@Cu-IIP-A fluorescence quenching of copper ions
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensitivity and stability determine whether the material can be utilized for fluorescence detection. Therefore, the capacity tube was equipped with CD@Cu-IIP-A suspension and different concentrations of copper ion solution (80–780 μmol L-1), in which the concentration of CD@Cu-IIP-A in the mixed solution was 0.08 mg L-1 was uniformly dispersed and static 15 min. The fluorescence intensity was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer and measured 3 times under the same conditions and averaged.

In order to investigate the effect of adding CD on the adsorption properties of materials, 6 kinds of materials: 4 ion-imprinted CD@Cu-IIP-A, CD@Cu-IIP-B, Cu-IIP-A, Cu-IIP-B and 2 non-imprinted NIP-A and NIP-B without CD and template were dispersed into the aqueous solution containing copper ions. The isothermal and kinetic adsorption experiments were carried out with UV–vis spectrophotometer.

When researching the adsorption process, isothermal adsorption should be considered firstly, which could be exactly described how they interact with each other between analytes and adsorbent. In the isothermal adsorption experiment, 100 mL of different concentrations (3, 10, 30, 60, 100, and 150 mg L-1) of copper ion solutions were taken into the conical bottle, and IIP polymer (10 mg) was added to carry out isothermal adsorption experiments. After the conical bottle was oscillated at constant temperature under the condition of 220 rpm for 3 h, the aqueous suspension was centrifuged, and the adsorbed copper ion solution was obtained through filter membrane of 0.22 μm. After adding the buffer and chromogenic agent, the absorbance was detected 3 times in the UV–vis spectrophotometer and averaged, and the adsorption amount was calculated according to Eq. (1).
(1) Qe=(C0-Ce) × V/m
where Q e (mg g−1) is equilibrium adsorption capacity, C 0 and C e (mg L-1) are the initial concentration and equilibrium concentration of copper ion, respectively, V (mL) is the volume of copper ion solution, and m (mg) is the mass of materials. The isothermal adsorption data are further analyzed by Langmuir Eq. (2), Freundlich Eq. (3) and Temkin Eq. (4) isothermal models. The formula is as follows:
(2)
C e/Q e = C e/Q max + 1/K L Q max
(3) lgQ e = lgC e/n + lgK F
(4)
Q e = K T1ln(K T2 C e)
where Q max (mg g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity, C e (mg mL−1) is the concentration in equilibrium state, 1/n is the empirical coefficient of Freundlich, K L (mL mg−1), K F (mg g−1) and K T are Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin constant, respectively.

Kinetic adsorption performance is the key factor to measure whether the material can quickly remove the target substance. In the dynamic adsorption experiment, 100 mL of copper ion solution with the concentration of 60 mg L-1 were placed in the conical bottle. After dispersing the material of 10 mg into the copper ion solution and oscillating different time (1, 5, 15, 30, 60, and 120 min) at constant temperature under the condition of 200 rpm, the suspension was centrifuged and then passed the adsorbed copper ion solution through the filter membrane of 0.22 μm. After adding the buffer and chromogenic agent, the absorbance was detected 3 times in the UV–vis spectrophotometer and averaged. The dynamic adsorption data were further analyzed by pseudo-first-order Eq. (5), pseudo-second-order Eq. (6) and intraparticle diffusion Eq. (7). Kinetic models with reference to the corresponding reports [48,49]. The formula is as follows:
(5) ln(Q e-Q t) = lnQ e-K 1t
(6)
t/Q t = t/Q e + 1/K 2 Q e 2
(7)
Q t = K I t 0.5 + C
where K 1, K 2 and K I are the reaction rate constants of pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and intraparticle diffusion kinetic model, respectively, Q t (mg g−1) is the adsorption capacity at a particular time.

Competitive adsorption experiments can verify whether a substance has excellent selective adsorption ability in complex environments. An aqueous solution containing 65 mg L-1 of copper ion and 65 mg L-1 of competitive metal ion (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+and Fe3+) was prepared. Then CD@Cu-IIP-A (12 mg) was added into the prepared aqueous solution. After the conical flask was shaken at 220 rpm for 3 h at constant temperature, the supernatant was passed through 0.45 μm filter membrane to obtain the adsorbed metal ion solution. Distribution ratio and selectivity coefficient of copper ion were calculated by Eq. (8) and Eq. (9). The formula is as follows:
(8)
K d=(C 0-C e) × V/(C e × m)
(9)
k = K d(Cu2+)/K d(Mn+)
where M represents other competitive metal ions, K d and k are the distribution ratio and selectivity coefficient, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process of dual-functional copper ion imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: In ion imprinting technique, the addition of chelating reagents containing amino, amide or sulfonic acid groups can significantly improve the ability to chelate metal ions, which makes the prepared IIP have more abundant cavities, so as to improve the imprinting effect [50–52]. Among them, DPC has strong complexing ability to copper ion and was often utilized as a detection agent for metal ions. However, the shortcomings of IIP prepared by conventional bulk polymerization limit its application in the detection and enrichment of copper ions in water [53,54]. SI-ATRP, a type of controlled/living radical polymerization technique, is an effective way to overcome the issues of recognition sites being embedded and/or having a heterogeneous distribution [40]. In order to conquer the deficiency of amorphous IIP, herein, amino-containing CD was prepared by o-phenylenediamine and tryptophan and integrated into spherical IIP by ATRP, making it multifunctional.

As shown in Fig. 1 , monodisperse spherical poly(GMA-co-EDMA) resin was first prepared via one-step seed swelling polymerization, and its surface was modified via SI-ATRP by adding DPC, MAA, EDMA or Cu2+. Among them, both DPC and CD containing amino group could chelate with Cu2+, while MAA not only interacted with DPC and CD through hydrogen bond, but also copolymerized with EDMA to generate an imprinting layer on the surface of resin (CD@Cu-IIP-A). Similarly, another kind of IIP was also prepared by bulk polymerization of the same functional monomer and crosslinker, and the as-synthesized product was ground and sieved to obtain a suitable amorphous material for adsorption (CD@Cu-IIP-B). For comparison of their functionality, both spherical Cu-IIP-A and amorphous Cu-IIP-B were fabricated without adding CD, as well as both spherical NIP-A and amorphous NIP-B were fabricated without adding the template (Cu2+).

The morphologies of two kinds of IIPs were evaluated through SEM. It could be observed that MAR with an average particle size of about 5 μm was successfully prepared by seed swelling polymerization (Fig. 2 a and b), and the particles of both Cu-IIP-A (Fig. 2c and d) and CD@Cu-IIP-A (Fig. 2e and f) fabricated with SI-ATRP still kept similar morphology and size to those of pristine MAR. Obviously, both Cu-IIP-B and CD@Cu-IIP-B prepared by bulk polymerization possessed irregular shape and wide size distribution of Cu-IIP-B and CD@Cu-IIP-B, ranging from hundreds of nanometers to several microns (Fig. S1).

FT-IR is usually adopted to characterize functional groups on the surface of materials. The FT-IR spectra of MAR-Br, CD@Cu-IIP-A and NIP-A are presented in Fig. 3 a. It could be observed that the stretch vibration of –OH groups is located at 3544 cm−1 in the spectrum of MAR-Br, and the adsorption peak of -C = O group at 1729 cm−1. These peaks still emerged in the spectra of CD@Cu-IIP-A and NIP-A, in which a broad peak at 3600–3200 cm−1 should be assigned as the stretch vibration of –NH and –OH groups. The characteristic peaks at 1160 and 1620 cm−1 were assigned N–H groups and C = C unsaturated bonds, respectively, which was due to the introduction of DPC in the material, indicating the success of the preparation of copper ion imprinted polymer.

The thermal stability of MAR-Br, CD@Cu-IIP-A and CD@Cu-IIP-B by TGA are shown in Fig. 3b. It could be seen that 3 kinds of materials exhibited better thermal stability and less weight loss before 240 °C. After 240 °C, the weights of MAR-Br and CD@Cu-IIP-A were lost sharply, but the weight of CD@Cu-IIP-B was slowly lost. The difference is attributed to the fact that both MAR-Br and CD@Cu-IIP-A are dominated by pristine poly(GMA-co-EDMA) MAR, while CD@Cu-IIP-B is composed of DPC, MAA and EDMA. When the temperature was 360–460 °C, the weight loss of 3 materials increased further. The weight of CD@Cu-IIP-B rapidly decreased, which was speculated to be the skeleton of the materials and the oxidative decomposition of DPC and other components, while the weight loss rate of CD@Cu-IIP-A and MAR-Br relatively slowed down, When the temperature reached 470 °C, the weight changes of the 3 materials stabilized, and there was no longer decrease of weight, presumably because the samples were completely carbonized. In the interval of 460–800 °C, it could be observed that the weight of them was still slowly decreased. The final weight loss of CD@Cu-IIP-A was 89.96%, which was less than 95.20% of CD@Cu-IIP-B, because the unique structure of the former was different from that of the latter acquired via bulk polymerization. Additionally, the weight loss of CD@Cu-IIP-A was slightly higher than that of MAR-Br (87.7%), because of the decomposition of the imprinting layer, further indicating successful preparation of CD@Cu-IIP-A via SI-ATRP.

The determination of elemental composition of CD@Cu-IIP-A before and after adsorption of Cu2+ was carried out by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3c, carbon, oxygen and nitrogen atoms were observed in CD@Cu-IIP-A before adsorption. The absence of copper elements in CD@Cu-IIP-A before adsorption indicated that the copper ion as template was completely removed from polymeric matrix. After adsorption, copper atom was observed in CD@Cu-IIP-A, indicating that CD@Cu-IIP-A successfully adsorbed copper ions.

The results of elemental analysis are shown in Table S1. Compared with pristine MAR, the carbon content of CD@Cu-IIP-A obviously decreased from 58.87% to 49.40%, the content of hydrogen decreased from 7.22% to 6.38%, but the proportion of nitrogen slightly increased from 0.15% to 0.16%, which was due to the introduction of DPC, MAA and EDMA in the polymerization. DPC contains high content of nitrogen, the introduction of which would increase the content of nitrogen in CD@Cu-IIP-A, and concomitantly reduce the content of carbon and hydrogen. The nitrogen content of CD@Cu-IIP-B was 0.44%, which was higher than 0.16% of CD@Cu-IIP-A, because more DPC with high nitrogen content were added into the polymerization system.

The specific surface area of CD@Cu-IIP-A and NIP-A were analyzed by nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiments (BET), and the results are shown in Fig. S1e. Due to the imprinting effect, CD@Cu-IIP-A (12.64 m2 g−1) exhibited greater surface area than NIP-A (7.41 m2 g−1). The imprinting of copper ions provided a complementary spatial structure for the CD@Cu-IIP-A to selectively identify the target.

The adsorption properties of IIP and NIP can be evaluated by adsorption isotherms with batch experiments, as shown in Fig. 4 . Through the observation of Fig. 4a, it could be seen that the Cu2+ adsorption for Cu-IIP-A and CD@Cu-IIP-A rapidly increased with an increase of copper ion concentration (less than 60 mg L-1). When the copper ion concentration was about 100 mg L-1, the adsorption equilibrium was reached. The maximum adsorption capacity of CD@Cu-IIP-A was 85.6 mg g−1, which was slightly lower than that of Cu-IIP-A (91.9 mg g−1), while the maximum adsorption of NIP-A was about 29.2 mg g−1 (Fig. 4a). Thus the imprinting factor (I = Q MIP/Q NIP [42]) could be calculated to be 3.14 and 2.93 for CD@Cu-IIP-A and Cu-IIP-A, respectively. It is observed from Fig. 4b that there was a similar trend in the adsorption curves of Cu-IIP-B and CD@Cu-IIP-B, and the adsorption amount of Cu2+ rapidly increased with the increase of copper ion concentration (less than 60 mg L-1). The maximum adsorption capacity of Cu-IIP-B synthesized by bulk polymerization was 70.5 mg g−1, that of CD@Cu-IIP-B was 66.1 mg g−1, but that of NIP-B was about 34.2 mg g−1 (Fig. 4b). The imprinting factors of 2.06 and 1.93 were acquired for CD@Cu-IIP-B and Cu-IIP-B, respectively. Thus, the spherical IIPs (CD@Cu-IIP-A and Cu-IIP-A) were more suitable for adsorption of copper ions in an aqueous solution environment. Through fitting equilibrium adsorption isotherm, as presented in Fig. S2 and Table S2, the results showed that the adsorption isotherm of Langmuir model had higher fitting degree and higher correlation coefficient than Freundlich and Temkin models. The results show that the six materials are mainly monolayer adsorption. The results indicated that monolayer adsorption was mainly carried out on the surfaces of these 6 materials.

The kinetic adsorption characteristics of copper ion by 6 materials were studied. The kinetic adsorption showed that the adsorption amount rapidly increased within initial 30 min, and then the increase of adsorption amount gradually slowed down (Fig. 4c-d). Specifically, the spherical IIP (Cu-IIP-A and CD@Cu-IIP-A) reached the adsorption equilibrium in 30–60 min (Fig. 4c), where the equilibrium adsorption capacity was 64.5 mg g−1 for Cu-IIP-A, 72.7 mg g−1 for CD@Cu-IIP-A. The average adsorption rate of CD@Cu-IIP-A was 2.135 mg g−1 min−1 in the first 30 min and 0.096 mg g−1 min−1 in 30–120 min, indicating that CD@Cu-IIP-A was close to adsorption equilibrium within 30 min. The amorphous IIP (Cu-IIP-B and CD@Cu-IIP-B) reached the adsorption equilibrium at about 60 min (Fig. 4d), where the equilibrium adsorption capacity was 59.4 mg g−1 for Cu-IIP-B, 62.4 mg g−1 for CD@Cu-IIP-B. Obviously, spherical IIP took shorter time to reach adsorption equilibrium than amorphous IIP. Pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and intraparticle diffusion kinetic models were adopted to fit these materials. As presented in Fig. S3 and Table S3, the correlation coefficients (R2) of pseudo-second-order model for 6 materials were higher than the correlation coefficients (R1 and RI) of the pseudo-first-order and intraparticle diffusion models. It could be seen that the experimental data fitted well with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model, indicating that chemical adsorption was mainly carried out on its surfaces. It is worth mentioning that pseudo-first-order also had this very high fit coefficient, which suggested be a combination of multiple types of adsorption types.

The effect of doped CD on the adsorption properties of spherical IIPs (CD@Cu-IIP-A and Cu-IIP-A) was analyzed. In isothermal adsorption, it could be aware that the maximum adsorption capacity of CD@Cu-IIP-A did not decrease significantly after doping with CD. The addition of CD allowed the IIP to gain more ability to fluorescently detect copper ion, while having little effect on its maximum adsorption capacity. The similar phenomenon was observed in amorphous CD@Cu-IIP-B and Cu-IIP-B. In kinetic adsorption, as shown in Fig. 4c, the adsorption of CD@Cu-IIP-A significantly increased faster than that of Cu-IIP-A within 30 min, which indicated that the addition of CD facilitated to reach adsorption equilibrium, possibly due to the interaction between CD and copper ion. Thus CD@Cu-IIP-A was more likely to fast adsorb copper ion from lower concentration aqueous solution.

The adsorption properties of spherical and amorphous IIPs (CD@Cu-IIP-A versus CD@Cu-IIP-B, Cu-IIP-A versus Cu-IIP-B) were also compared. The isothermal adsorption experiment showed that the maximum adsorption capacity of spherical Cu-IIP-A synthesized by ATRP modification of MAR was 91.9 mg g−1, which was higher than that of amorphous Cu-IIP-B prepared by conventional bulk polymerization (70.5 mg g−1). Additionally, the adsorption equilibrium of the former was shorter than that of the latter. We speculated that there are two main reasons for the difference between them. First, bulk polymerization was uncontrollable, the degree of polymerization was uneven, and grinding would destroy part of recognition sites, resulting in a reduction of adsorption capacity. Second, the bulk materials exhibited lower dispersity in water than spherical materials. As shown in Fig. S4, CD@Cu-IIP-A could disperse stably in water after 1 h, while CD@Cu-IIP-B gradually sank after 5 min. This made fluorescence intensity unstable when performing fluorescence quenching experiment of CD@Cu-IIP-B, limiting the quantitative detection of copper ions by CD@Cu-IIP-B.

The performance of the two representative materials CD@Cu-IIP-A and CD@Cu-IIP-B prepared in this work and a few previously reported IIPs of copper ions are listed in Table 1 . Dahaghin et al. reported the preparation of a selective magnetic Fe3O4@SiO2@IIP through isatin (Isa) as a ligand and 4-vinylpyridine as a functional monomer [55], its maximum adsorption capacity was 83.0 mg g−1, allowing a rapid separation of the adsorbent. Ren et al. prepared an adsorption composite via ion imprinted technique in conjunction with sol–gel process using N-[3-(2-aminoethylamino) propyl] trimethoxysilane as functional monomer and tetraethyl orthosilicate as cross-linker [56]. Water was used as a solvent throughout the preparation process, and the synthetic route was green and non-polluting, whose maximum adsorption capacity was 39.8 mg g−1 and the adsorption equilibrium was reached in 60 min. Li et al. synthesized a magnetic imprinted polymer with magnetic CNTs/Fe3O4 as the core, 3-ammonium propyltriethoxysilane as the functional monomer, tetraethylorthosilicate as the cross-linker [57]. Its maximum adsorption capacity was 42.2 mg g−1 and the adsorption equilibrium was reached in 50 min. Liu et al. synthesized a copper ion imprinted graphene oxide sheet with large surface area and applied in solid phase extraction, its maximum adsorption capacity was 109.4 mg g−1 and the adsorption equilibrium could be reached within 30 min [58]. Although the maximum adsorption capacity of CD@Cu-IIP-A was slightly lower than that of ion imprinted graphene oxide sheet, CD@Cu-IIP-A exhibited higher adsorption performance than other 3 kinds of IIP materials.

The imprinted cavities of IIPs provide specific recognition of the target ions due to their size and binding sites. The adsorption selectivity of CD@Cu-IIP-A was investigated with a binary mixture containing Cu2+ and one of interfering ions such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+and Fe3+, respectively. These ions may co-exist with copper ions in real wastewater. As listed in Table 2 , the selectivity coefficients k of all Cu2+/interfering ions were 2.54, 3.43, 1.58, 3.10, 1.47 (interfering ion was Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+and Fe3+, respectively). It could be observed that CD@Cu-IIP-A possessed different selectivity, with high selectivity for Na+, K+ and Ni2 (k > 2.5) and slightly poor selectivity for Mg2+ and Fe3+ (k less than 1.6). This phenomenon was related to the formation of specific imprinted cavities for CD@Cu-IIP-A during imprinting process, and thus had a better specific adsorption capacity for Cu2+ and similar ions.

The reusability of an adsorbent is an important indicator for practical application, and the adsorbent that can be adsorbed multiple times is stable and can effectively reduce the cost of adsorption. The rapid adsorption–desorption experiment was performed on CD@Cu-IIP-A (30 mg). Then the copper ions were eluted by centrifugal washing and ultrasonication with 0.1 mol L-1 hydrochloric acid. The eluted CD@CU-IIP-A was then reused under the same conditions for copper ion adsorption. The adsorption amounts measured in 5 cycles were 54.88, 51.68, 49.85, 42.53 and 39.79 mg g−1. It could be deduced that the elution process had less effect on the structure and chemical properties of the IIP cavity. So CD@Cu-IIP-A possessed good stability and regeneration properties.

It was deduced that the integration of CD allowed the CD@Cu-IIP-A to emit the fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the excitation wavelength of CD@Cu-IIP-A was 420 nm and the emission wavelength was 571 nm, which was in yellow fluorescence range. The quenching of Cu2+ by CD@Cu-IIP-A was investigated. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of CD@Cu-IIP-A decreased continuously with an increase of Cu2+ concentration, and there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 80–780 μmol L-1 (R = 0.993). The linear curve is shown in the Fig. 5b, using the 3σ rule (σ = S0/S, S0 is known as the standard deviation after 12 repeated measurements of the blank, and S is the slope of the calibration curve), the limit of detection (LOD) for CD@Cu-IIP-A was 3.17 μmol L-1. Thus, these results proved that CD@Cu-IIP-A possessed dual functions of both adsorption and fluorescent detection of Cu2+.

As described above, CD@Cu-IIP-B showed poor dispersion in water (Fig. S4) and could not generate stable fluorescent performance for detection. A considerable number of reports [59–61] has pointed out that the CD synthesized from o-phenylenediamine has the structure of large conjugated system and the amino, which decides their fluorescent properties. Meanwhile, the addition of tryptophan would introduce carboxyl and other functional groups, which can absorb copper ions. When fluorescence quenching occurs, there are two different quenching mechanisms. On the one hand, the inherent paramagnetism of copper ions will affect the charge transfer of carboxyl groups on CD, resulting in fluorescence quenching [62,63]. On the other hand, the yellow fluorescence of CD in the water environment was probably related to the protonation of amino groups [59]. According to the yellow fluorescence of CD@Cu-IIP-A and the quenching of copper ion, it was deduced that more than one kind of imprinting cavity was generated in the process of synthesizing CD@Cu-IIP-A. The functional groups on DPC and CD interacted with copper ion and produced different types of imprinted cavities, respectively. This was also the reason for the difference between the adsorption properties of Cu-IIP-A and CD@Cu-IIP-A. So far, we successfully combined the efficient and specific adsorption of IIP with the fluorescence quenching detection of CD, thus copper ions in the aqueous environment could be detected and removed by the specific adsorption of CD@Cu-IIP-A.

In this study, using poly (GMA-co-EDMA) as matrix, copper ion-imprinted spherical CD@Cu-IIP-A has been successfully synthesized by adding ligand DPC, functional monomer MAA, cross-linking agent EDMA and yellowish fluorescent CD, which was prepared with tryptophan and o-phenylenediamine as precursors. The excitation wavelength of CD@Cu-IIP-A was 420 nm and the emission wavelength was 571 nm, which had good fluorescence quenching effect on copper ions. Compared with amorphous IIPs and spherical Cu-IIP-A, the material of CD@Cu-IIP-A exhibited following advantages. First, CD@Cu-IIP-A possessed excellent adsorption performance, which is significantly better than CD@Cu-IIP-B prepared by conventional bulk polymerization, while the adsorption equilibrium could be reached within 30 min to meet the conditions of copper ion detection in complex environments. Second, quantitative analysis of copper ions could be carried out by CD@Cu-IIP-A with stable fluorescence quenching, indicating a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and copper ion concentration within 80–780 μmol L-1 in aqueous solution environment. Third, the synthetic process of CD@Cu-IIP-A was green and had excellent dispersity in an aqueous environment, making it ideal for the adsorption and detection of copper ions in real environment. In a word, CD@Cu-IIP-A not only exhibited excellent adsorption ability, but also possessed the ability to detect copper ions by fluorescent spectroscopy. This provides a new idea for the detection and removal of heavy metal pollution in water environment at the same time. It can be predicted that new CD-ion imprinting materials with both specific recognition and fluorescent detection will become a new way for rapid detection and removal of heavy metal ions in water environment.
